expectations.text: problems.md explicitly names 'utils.py' as a dumping-ground / generic filename problem
expectations.evidence: Section 1 is titled 'Dumping-ground filename (AR003)' and explicitly calls out `utils.py` as on the banlist with purpose unclear from name.

expectations.text: problems.md calls out the generic class name 'Manager' as a naming problem
expectations.evidence: Table row 4 lists `class Manager` stating 'Noun Manager on the banlist' as a naming problem.

expectations.text: problems.md identifies __getattr__ / dynamic dispatch (the DynamicAPI class) as a problem for AI agents
expectations.evidence: Row 13 of section 4 names `DynamicAPI.__getattr__` saying 'Every attribute access synthesizes a no-op lambda. No method a tool can jump to.'

expectations.text: problems.md flags the use of eval() as an anti-pattern
expectations.evidence: Section 4 row 11 lists `eval(expr)` as 'Arbitrary string evaluation. Grep for foo( will never find calls that pass through eval.'

expectations.text: problems.md identifies the deep inheritance chain (Base -> Entity -> Persisted -> Auditable -> User)
expectations.evidence: Section 5 explicitly shows 'User -> Auditable -> Persisted -> Entity -> Base (depth 4, limit 3)'.

expectations.text: problems.md notes that public functions lack type annotations / return types
expectations.evidence: Section 3 'Missing type annotations at public boundaries (AR006)' lists 5 signatures with 'No param types, no return type.'

expectations.text: refactored.py's public functions have type annotations on parameters and return values
expectations.evidence: All public functions like `normalize_email(email: str) -> str`, `sum_event_counts(a: int, b: int) -> int`, and `format_audit_event(event: AuditEvent, format_name: str) -> str` have full annotations.

expectations.text: refactored.py does not use __getattr__ for runtime method dispatch
expectations.evidence: No `__getattr__` appears in the refactored code; the docstring explicitly states 'DynamicAPI.__getattr__ deleted.'

expectations.text: refactored.py does not call eval() or exec() on user input
expectations.evidence: No `eval(` or `exec(` call appears in the refactor; docstring notes 'eval and importlib.import_module helpers were removed.'

expectations.text: refactored.py replaces the generic 'Manager' class name with something domain-specific (or removes it)
expectations.evidence: Manager is removed; its behavior is moved to the domain class `AuditTrail.record` as noted in the docstring.

expectations.text: refactored.py's inheritance chain is at most 2 levels deep (or uses composition instead)
expectations.evidence: User is a flat `@dataclass` that composes `AuditTrail` via a field; no inheritance chain remains.

expectations.text: rationale.md grounds recommendations in specific AI-agent failure modes (e.g. grep misses, hallucination, str_replace breakage) rather than generic clean-code arguments
expectations.evidence: Each section cites concrete failure modes: 'SweRank 2025 identifies file localization as the #1 bottleneck', 'Liu et al. 2025 measured a drop from 87.3% -> 58.7%', 'ETH SRI Fixing Correct Code postmortem', and 'Surge HQ 693-line-spiral postmortem'.

expectations.text: layout.txt organizes by feature (auth/, billing/, users/) rather than primarily by horizontal layer (controllers/, services/, models/ as top-level)
expectations.evidence: Layout uses src/features/{auth,users,billing}/ with no top-level controllers/services/models directories.

expectations.text: layout.txt colocates test files next to source files (*.test.ts or *.spec.ts adjacent to source) rather than segregating them into a distant tests/ tree
expectations.evidence: Each feature dir contains colocated tests like auth.routes.test.ts and auth.service.test.ts next to source files.

expectations.text: layout.txt contains concrete domain directories like auth, billing, or users (not just generic scaffold)
expectations.evidence: src/features/ contains concrete auth/, users/, and billing/ directories with domain-specific files.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md explicitly addresses naming — e.g. avoiding generic names (Manager/Service/Handler) or requiring domain-specific names
expectations.evidence: Section 2 bans name fragments Manager, Service (class suffix), Helper, Util, Handler, Common, Misc and requires verbs with domain objects.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md gives a concrete file-size guideline (a number, e.g. under 500 or 800 lines)
expectations.evidence: Section 3 states 'Hard ceiling: 400 lines per file. Soft target: 250.'

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md requires type annotations on public / module-boundary functions
expectations.evidence: Section 5: 'Public function signatures are fully typed. No any, no implicit return types on exported functions.'

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md mentions tests/lint/typecheck as the verification loop (colocated tests, a single bash command, or similar)
expectations.evidence: Section 7: 'One verifiable command: bun run check runs typecheck + lint + tests.'

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md discourages dynamic/metaprogramming patterns (reflection, runtime dispatch, heavy DI frameworks) — or equivalent — for agent-traceability reasons
expectations.evidence: Section 4 forbids Proxy, Reflect.defineProperty, runtime monkey-patching, dynamic imports of app modules, DI containers, with rationale 'Control flow must be visible to grep.'

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md explains at least one decision in terms of AI-agent behavior (grep-ability, context windows, str_replace, hallucination) rather than generic clean-code reasoning
expectations.evidence: Multiple agent-centric justifications, e.g. 'Files over 400 reliably break apply-model edits and burn context' and 'a class hierarchy the agent will fabricate methods into.'

expectations.text: recommendations.md discusses naming with explicit reference to grep-ability, retrieval, or localization (not just 'use good names')
expectations.evidence: Section 1 is 'Name things for localization' and states 'Agents localize code by grepping names... Aider's repo-map and Cursor's retrieval both rank code by symbol references' and '`grep refund` now returns one file'.

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends organizing code by feature / vertical slice rather than by horizontal layer
expectations.evidence: Section 2 is explicitly titled 'Colocate by feature (vertical slices), not by technical layer' and contrasts src/billing/refunds/ against controllers/services/middleware layering.

expectations.text: recommendations.md warns against metaprogramming, reflection, or dynamic dispatch and explains why it hurts agents specifically
expectations.evidence: Section 6 'Prefer static, grep-traceable dependencies over metaprogramming' names __getattr__, eval, Proxy, Reflect and cites ETH SRI Lab / DAPLab evidence that agents hallucinate around untraceable code.

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends typed public boundaries / type annotations at module seams
expectations.evidence: Section 3 says 'Annotate every public function signature. Types are the contract retrievers and constrained-generation systems trust' and Section 10 covers validating at module boundaries with Zod/Pydantic.

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends verification affordances (colocated tests, single command for lint+test+typecheck, fast feedback) and ties this to preventing agent hallucination spirals
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'Give the agent a verification loop' cites the Surge HQ 'Gemini produced 693 lines of wrong patches over 39 turns' spiral and prescribes colocated tests plus one Makefile/justfile command for lint+typecheck+tests.

expectations.text: recommendations.md addresses file size, near-duplicates, or apply-model / str_replace breakage
expectations.evidence: Section 5 explicitly discusses Morph LLM apply-model failures on >1,000 line files and 'Claude Code's `str_replace` and Aider's SEARCH/REPLACE both require unique context lines' breaking on duplicates.

expectations.text: recommendations.md grounds the reasoning in specific agent failure modes (e.g. mid-context position bias, grep misses, str_replace ambiguity, hallucinated APIs, spiral edits) rather than generic 'clean code' platitudes
expectations.evidence: Cites 'lost in the middle' position bias (Liu et al. 2023), str_replace/SEARCH-REPLACE ambiguity, Gemini 693-line hallucination spiral, grep misses on __getattr__, and SWE-Bench Pro multi-file edit failure rates.

expectations.text: recommendations.md presents the list in a prioritized order (top 3, ranked list, or similar) rather than flat unordered bullets
expectations.evidence: The document is divided into 'Priority 1 — The non-negotiables', 'Priority 2 — Structural rules', 'Priority 3 — Meta-level scaffolding' with numbered items 1-10 and a final 'Fix in this order' ordering.

expectations.text: problems.md explicitly names 'utils.py' as a dumping-ground / generic filename problem
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'Filename carries no semantic information' names `utils.py` and says the intro calls it a 'dumping-ground module'.

expectations.text: problems.md calls out the generic class name 'Manager' as a naming problem
expectations.evidence: Section 2 lists 'Manager, Base, Entity' among generic, semantically empty identifiers.

expectations.text: problems.md identifies __getattr__ / dynamic dispatch (the DynamicAPI class) as a problem for AI agents
expectations.evidence: Section 5 bullet calls out 'DynamicAPI.__getattr__ returning lambda **kw: None — every attribute access succeeds with a no-op.'

expectations.text: problems.md flags the use of eval() as an anti-pattern
expectations.evidence: Section 5 first bullet: 'eval(expr) — arbitrary string execution... It is also a security hole.'

expectations.text: problems.md identifies the deep inheritance chain (Base -> Entity -> Persisted -> Auditable -> User)
expectations.evidence: Section 6 'Deep, empty inheritance chain' writes out 'User -> Auditable -> Persisted -> Entity -> Base, five levels, every intermediate class empty.'

expectations.text: problems.md notes that public functions lack type annotations / return types
expectations.evidence: Section 3 'Missing type annotations on public API' lists five functions with 'no parameter types and no return type.'

expectations.text: refactored.py's public functions have type annotations on parameters and return values
expectations.evidence: Public functions such as `add_integers(left: int, right: int) -> int`, `normalize_user_payload(payload: Mapping[str, object]) -> dict[str, object]`, and `safe_eval_arithmetic(expression: str) -> int | float` all have full annotations.

expectations.text: refactored.py does not use __getattr__ for runtime method dispatch
expectations.evidence: The file contains no `__getattr__` and the rationale/comments state 'Removed DynamicAPI.__getattr__ entirely.'

expectations.text: refactored.py does not call eval() or exec() on user input
expectations.evidence: No `eval(` or `exec(` call appears; `safe_eval_arithmetic` uses `ast.parse` with a walker and allow-listed operators.

expectations.text: refactored.py replaces the generic 'Manager' class name with something domain-specific (or removes it)
expectations.evidence: Manager is removed; the rationale notes 'do_stuff → removed; its work is User.rename / User.deactivate / User.touch.'

expectations.text: refactored.py's inheritance chain is at most 2 levels deep (or uses composition instead)
expectations.evidence: `User` is a single flat `@dataclass(slots=True)` with no base classes, collapsing the original 5-level chain.

expectations.text: rationale.md grounds recommendations in specific AI-agent failure modes (e.g. grep misses, hallucination, str_replace breakage) rather than generic clean-code arguments
expectations.evidence: Rationale explains failure modes such as 'agents will pile type-checks, I/O, and validation into it because process accepts anything', 'Dynamic imports defeat rename refactors', and 'An agent writing tests against such an API will produce passing tests for non-existent methods.'

expectations.text: layout.txt organizes by feature (auth/, billing/, users/) rather than primarily by horizontal layer (controllers/, services/, models/ as top-level)
expectations.evidence: src/features/ contains auth/, users/, billing/ subfolders; there is no top-level controllers/ or models/ directory (middleware/ and lib/ are supporting, not primary layering).

expectations.text: layout.txt colocates test files next to source files (*.test.ts or *.spec.ts adjacent to source) rather than segregating them into a distant tests/ tree
expectations.evidence: Each feature folder includes a colocated auth.test.ts / users.test.ts / billing.test.ts alongside source (E2E tests in tests/e2e are a separate category).

expectations.text: layout.txt contains concrete domain directories like auth, billing, or users (not just generic scaffold)
expectations.evidence: src/features/ contains concrete auth/, users/, billing/ directories with domain-named files.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md explicitly addresses naming — e.g. avoiding generic names (Manager/Service/Handler) or requiring domain-specific names
expectations.evidence: Section 4 requires verb-first exports ('createUser'), specifies kebab-case files, and rejects names like 'handlePost' or generic 'UserSchema'.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md gives a concrete file-size guideline (a number, e.g. under 500 or 800 lines)
expectations.evidence: Section 3: '300 lines per file is the soft limit... 500 lines is a hard ceiling. CI fails above this.'

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md requires type annotations on public / module-boundary functions
expectations.evidence: Section 6 emphasizes inferring types from Zod/Drizzle but does not explicitly require annotated signatures on exported/public functions.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md mentions tests/lint/typecheck as the verification loop (colocated tests, a single bash command, or similar)
expectations.evidence: Testing section mentions 'bun test' and colocation, but there is no single verification command combining typecheck + lint + tests as the agent feedback loop.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md discourages dynamic/metaprogramming patterns (reflection, runtime dispatch, heavy DI frameworks) — or equivalent — for agent-traceability reasons
expectations.evidence: No explicit prohibition of Proxy, Reflect, dynamic imports, or DI containers; the conventions focus on layering and imports but do not address metaprogramming.

expectations.text: CONVENTIONS.md explains at least one decision in terms of AI-agent behavior (grep-ability, context windows, str_replace, hallucination) rather than generic clean-code reasoning
expectations.evidence: Multiple agent-centric rationales, e.g. 'Large files defeat agents: context windows fill with irrelevant code' and 'agent search (grep createUser) returns the definition...'

expectations.text: recommendations.md discusses naming with explicit reference to grep-ability, retrieval, or localization (not just 'use good names')
expectations.evidence: Section 4 says 'Name things so that a grep for the feature name finds everything. If a feature is called "checkout", every file related to it should contain that string.'

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends organizing code by feature / vertical slice rather than by horizontal layer
expectations.evidence: Section 5 'Collocate code, tests, types, and schema' recommends putting foo.ts/foo.test.ts/foo.types.ts/foo.schema.ts in the same directory rather than separate /tests, /types, /schemas trees.

expectations.text: recommendations.md warns against metaprogramming, reflection, or dynamic dispatch and explains why it hurts agents specifically
expectations.evidence: Section 4 says 'Avoid decorators / metaclasses / monkey-patching for core behavior' and 'Avoid magic strings that map to handlers' because agents follow imports and reimplement code they can't grep.

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends typed public boundaries / type annotations at module seams
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'Make the type system the contract' prescribes strict TS, mypy --strict, and 'Validate all external inputs at the boundary with Zod / Pydantic / Valibot so that internal code has real types'.

expectations.text: recommendations.md recommends verification affordances (colocated tests, single command for lint+test+typecheck, fast feedback) and ties this to preventing agent hallucination spirals
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Give the agent a fast, deterministic feedback loop' prescribes 'one command that does type-check + lint + unit tests in under ~30 seconds' and notes agents 'will confidently invent APIs that "look right" when they cannot verify', turning hallucination into a compile failure.

expectations.text: recommendations.md addresses file size, near-duplicates, or apply-model / str_replace breakage
expectations.evidence: Section 6 addresses file size ('A 2000-line file blows the context window') but does not mention near-duplicate blocks breaking str_replace / SEARCH-REPLACE / apply-model edits specifically.

expectations.text: recommendations.md grounds the reasoning in specific agent failure modes (e.g. mid-context position bias, grep misses, str_replace ambiguity, hallucinated APIs, spiral edits) rather than generic 'clean code' platitudes
expectations.evidence: Names concrete failure modes such as 'agents confidently invent APIs', 'any propagates silently', 'agents follow imports... if they can't find a symbol via grep... they assume it doesn't exist and reimplement it', and 'context window blows' on 2000-line files.

expectations.text: recommendations.md presents the list in a prioritized order (top 3, ranked list, or similar) rather than flat unordered bullets
expectations.evidence: Structured as 'Tier 1 — Highest leverage', 'Tier 2 — High leverage', 'Tier 3 — Meaningful polish' with numbered items 1-20 and a final 'minimal checklist you can apply this week'.
